{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q112025]] Wikidata name: Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese martial art}} {{Use dmy dates|date=February 2020}} {{infobox koryu | school = Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū
(天真正伝香取神道流) | image = Kenjyutsu-2.jpg | imagecaption = Meiji Shrine festival special dedication | founder = [[:en:Iizasa Ienao|Iizasa Chōisai Ienao]] (飯篠 長威斉 家直, c.1387–c.1488) | date founded = c.1447 | period founded = Middle [[Muromachi period]] (1336–1573) | location = [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]] | headmaster = Yasusada Iizasa (飯篠 修理亮 快貞 ''Iizasa Shūri-no-Suke Yasusada'', born c.20th century) | headquarters = [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]] | art1 = ''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]'' | description1 = Sword art | art2 = ''[[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]'' | description2 = Art of drawing the sword | art3 = ''[[Ryōtōjutsu]]'' | description3 = Art of [[dual-wield]]ing swords (usu. one long one short) | art4 = ''[[:en:Bōjutsu|Bōjutsu]]'' | description4 = Staff art | art5 = ''[[:en:Naginatajutsu|Naginatajutsu]]'' | description5 = [[:en:Glaive|Glaive]] art | art6 = ''[[:en:Sōjutsu|Sōjutsu]]'' | description6 = Spear art | art7 = ''[[:en:Shurikenjutsu|Shurikenjutsu]]'' | description7 = [[Dart (missile)|Dart]] throwing art | art8 = [[:en:Jujutsu|Jujutsu]] | description8 = [[Hybrid martial arts|Hybrid]] art, unarmed or with minor weapons | ancestors = Katori no Ken, Kashima no Ken | assimilated = | descendants = {{hlist| [[Nakamura-ryū]] | [[Meifu Shinkage ryu|Meifu Shinkage-ryū]] | [[Shintō Musō-ryū]] | [[Yagyū Shinkage-ryū]] }} }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū'''|天真正伝香取神道流}}Written as 天眞正傳香取神道流 before adoption (1946) of [[:en:Tōyō kanji|Tōyō kanji]]. is one of the oldest extant [[:en:Japanese martial arts|Japanese martial arts]] and an exemplar of ''[[:en:bujutsu|bujutsu]]''. It was founded by [[:en:Iizasa Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]], who lived near [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] ([[:en:Sawara, Chiba|Sawara City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]) at the time. The ''[[:en:ryu (school)|ryū]]'' is purported to have been founded in 1447, but some scholars state that it was about 1480.Watatani, Kiyoshi (1967). Zusetsu Kobudōshi, TokyoThe year 1387 is given as Iizasa's birth year in ''Deity and the Sword'', Vol 1 pp. 16–17. Watatani (1967) speculates that 1417–1420 is correct. ==History== ===Foundation=== [[File:Katori-shinto-ryu,doujyou-training-hall,katori-city,japan.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Katori Shinto Ryu Dojo training hall in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori]] city, [[:en:Japan|Japan]]]] [[File:Iizasa_Chōisai_Ienao.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Iizasa Chōisai Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]] founded Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū in 1447]] [[:en:Iizasa Chōisai Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]] (飯篠 長威斎 家直 ''Iizasa Chōi-sai Ienao'', {{Circa}} 1387 – c. 1488) was a respected [[:en:Sōjutsu|spearman]] and [[:en:swordsman|swordsman]] whose ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' was deposed, which encouraged him to relinquish control of his household to conduct purification rituals and study martial arts in isolation.Amdur, Ellis (2002). ''Old School: Essays on Japanese Martial Traditions'', Edgework, p. 21–45 Iizasa was born in the village of Iizasa in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]. When he was young, he moved to the vicinity of the famous [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]], northeast of Tokyo in modern-day [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The shrine's ''[[kami]]'', [[Futsunushi]] ({{nihongo| 経津主神 |Futsunushi no kami}}) is revered as a spirit of swordsmanship and martial arts. ===Headmasters=== In 1896, the 18th ''[[:en:Sōke|Sōke]]'' died without a male heir. ''[[:en:Shihan|Shihan]]'' Yamaguchi Eikan governed the school until his death on 14 March 1917. Until Iizasa Kinjiro married into the Iizasa household, the school was headed in order by nine instructors—Tamai Kisaburo, Shiina Ichizo, Ito Tanekichi, Kuboki Sazaemon, Isobe Kohei, Motomiya (Hongu) Toranosuke, Hayashi Yazaemon, Kamagata Minosuke, and the main instructor Yamaguchi.Sugino, Yoshio (2016). ''Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu: Budo Kyohan'', Ulf Rott ===Recent history=== [[File:Risuke_Ōtake.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Risuke Otake|Risuke Otake]] (Pic. M. Stokhof 2007)]]On 6 March 1960, the school received the first ever "Intangible Cultural Asset" designation given to a martial art by the Japanese government, naming Hayashi Yazaemon, [[:en:Risuke Otake|Ōtake Risuke]], and Iizasa Yasusada as its guardians. The Iizasa family dojo was also designated a Cultural Asset. The designation of Cultural Asset status shifted to the Chiba Prefectural Government in 1985 and the art was recertified, again naming Ōtake Risuke and Iizasa Yasusada as guardians.Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition''. Koryu Books. p 22-23. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-21-3}} Ōtake Nobutoshi and Kyōsō Shigetoshi were also certified as guardians on 30 March 2004.{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p211-005.html|title = 武術 天真正伝香取神道流}} Iizasa Yasusada has publicly recognised the Otake and Sugino dojo. Both dojo represent the school annually at the Meiji Jingu embu. Kyōsō Shigetoshi was appointed shihan in September 2017,{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/instruction/|title=Instruction – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto Ryu}} and Ōtake Nobutoshi was issued hamon (excommunicated) on 9 December 2018.{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/important-message/|title=Important Message – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto Ryu}} However, both Otake Risuke, Otake Nobutoshi and Kyōsō Shigetoshi are still recognised as guardians of the school, by the Chiba Prefecture Board of Cultural Affairs.{{Cite web |date=20 April 2023 |title=天真正伝香取神道流 神武館 | Tenshinshō-den Katori Shintō-ryū Shinbukan Dōjō | 千葉県無形文化財 | 大竹利典 | Ōtake Risuke | 大竹信利 | Ōtake Nobutoshi - Introduction |url=https://shinbukan-katorishintoryu.org }} ===Gallery=== Kenjyutsu-2.jpg|Meiji Shrine annual festival dedication Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) Kenjyutsu-2a.jpg|Same as left, Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) Kenjyutsu-2b.jpg|Same as left, Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) == Curriculum == The Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū is a comprehensive martial system. Unlike modern martial arts such as [[:en:Kendo|Kendo]] or ''[[:en:Iaido|Iaido]]'', which concentrate on one specific area of training, a broad range of martial skills is studied.Skoss, Diane (editor) (1999). ''Sword & Spirit, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 2, p. 67–69. {{ISBN|1-890536-05-9}} The main emphasis of the school is ''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]''. A wide range of other weapons are taught as part of the curriculum, but the sword remains the central weapon. The primary curriculum includes:Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition''. Koryu Books. p 61. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-21-3}} {| border="1" cellpadding="2" cellspacing="0" ! colspan = "2" style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Arts''' ! style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Forms''' |- | rowspan = "8" style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Sword arts'''
(''tachijutsu'') | rowspan = "3" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Sword combat'''
(''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Sword''|表之太刀|Omote no Tachi}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of the Sword''|五行之太刀|Gogyō no Tachi}} |- | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Seven Essential Articles of the Sword''|極意七条之太刀|Gokui Shichijo no Tachi}} |- | rowspan = "3" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Sword drawing'''
(''[[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of Sword-Drawing''|表之居合|Omote-no Iai}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Standing Sword-Drawing''|立合抜刀術|Tachiai [[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Sword Drawing''|極意之居合|Gokui no Iai}} |- | style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Two swords'''
(両刀術 ''ryōtōjutsu'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Two Swords''|両刀|Ryōtō}} |- | style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Short sword'''
(''[[:en:Kodachi|Kodachi]]'') | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of the Short Sword''|極意之小太刀|Gokui no Kodachi}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Staff art'''
(''[[:en:Bōjutsu|Bōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Staff''|表之棒|Omote no Bō}} |- | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of the Staff''|五行之棒|Gogyō no Bō}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''[[:en:Glaive|Glaive]] art'''
(''[[:en:Naginatajutsu|Naginatajutsu]]'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Glaive''|表之長刀|Omote no Naginata}} |- | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Seven Essential Articles of the Glaive''|極意七条之長刀|Gokui Shichijo no Naginata}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Spear art'''
(''[[:en:Sōjutsu|Sōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Spear''|表之槍|Omote no Yari}} |- | 2 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Secret forms''||Hiden no Yari}} |- | rowspan = "3" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Spike-throwing'''
(''[[:en:Shurikenjutsu|Shurikenjutsu]]'') | 7 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of Spike Throwing''|表之手裏剣|Omote no Shuriken}} |- | 8 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of Spike Throwing''|五行之手裏剣|Gogyō no Shuriken}} |- | 9 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Spike Throwing''|極意之手裏剣|Gokui no Shuriken}} |- | colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''[[:en:Jujutsu|Jujutsu]]''' | 36 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Jujutsu''|極意之柔術|Gokui no Jūjutsu}} |} The Gogyo and Gokui kata are only taught to advanced practitioners after years of fundamental practice. Other, more advanced areas of study of the school include: *''Ninjutsu'' (intelligence gathering and analysis) *''Chikujojutsu'' (field fortification art) *''Gunbai-Heihō'' (strategy and tactics) *''Tenmon Chirigaku'' ([[:en:Chinese astrology|Chinese astrology]] and [[:en:Geomancy|geomantic divination]]) *''In-Yo kigaku'' (philosophical and mystical aspects derived from [[:en:Mikkyō|Mikkyō]], specifically [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]]) ==Keppan== Historically, before beginning any training in Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū, every prospective pupil had to swear an oath of allegiance to the school. The method was to make ''keppan'' (blood oath) in support of the following ''kisho'' or ''kishomon'' (pledge). This oath was a written one with the prospective member being required to sign his name in his own blood. The applicant would prick or cut a finger or sometimes the inner arm and with the blood drawn, sign the following pledge: Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition'', Koryu Books. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-20-6}} (A total revamp of ''The Deity and the Sword'', combining all 3 volumes into one re-translated and edited book with additional material. However, with far fewer photographs per kata as compared to the original 3 volumes. Similarly, all the original English explanatory notes and guidelines relating to each of the kata have been removed.)
On becoming a member of the Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū which has been transmitted by the Great Deity of the Katori Shrine, I herewith affirm my pledge that: #I will not have the impertinence to discuss or demonstrate details of the ''ryū'' to either non-members or members, even if they are relatives; #I will not engage in altercations or misuse the art against others; #I will never engage in any kind of gambling nor frequent disreputable places. #I will not cross swords with any followers of other martial traditions without authorization. I hereby pledge to firmly adhere to each of the above articles. Should I break any of these articles I will submit to the punishment of the Great Deity of Katori and the Great Deity Marishiten. Herewith I solemnly swear and affix my blood seal to this oath to these Great Deities.
==Branches== Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū is currently represented by the Kyōsō Shibu led by Kyōsō Shigetoshi, Ōtake Risuke's younger son. The honbu dojo is located at Iizasa Yasusada's home near the Katori shrine and is used by branches in good standing on special occasions. The Kyōsō Shibu regularly trains out of the Shisui Town Community Plaza and the Matsuyamashita Koen Sports Gymnasium in Inzai City.{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shinto-ryu.org/en/dojo/|title = Dojo – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto-ryu Kyoso Shibu}} Several branches have existed alongside or split from the mainline with varying ties to the Iizasa family, such as Noda, Yoseikan, Shinbukan, Sugino, Hatakeyama, Sugawara. ==Ranking and appointed positions== The different branches recognize different levels of Ranks and appointments. The traditional Ranks are a variant of the ''[[:en:menkyo|menkyo]]'' system.{{Cite web|url=http://judoinfo.com/Ranks/|title = Ranking Systems in Modern Japanese Martial Arts: Modern vs. Classical | Judo Info}} ===Ōtake=== ====Ranks==== * Mokuroku (目録, "catalog") * Menkyo (免許, "license, certificate") * Gokui Kaiden (極意皆伝, "deepest transmission") ====Appointments==== * Shidōsha (指導者, "mentor, coach"; given as license to teach outside of Shinbukan dojo) * Shihan (師範, 'instructor'; head-teacher) ===Sugino Dojo=== The Sugino line uses the modern [[Dan (Rank)|dan]] system, out of respect for Yoshio Sugino's (10th dan) judo teacher Kanō Jigorō, who was a pioneer of the dan-i Ranking system.{{Cite web|url=http://www.katsujinken.nl/2012/04/02/het-gradensysteem/|title = About the Katori Shinto ryu grading system | Katsujinken}} Sugino dojo members also receive densho/makimono. ===Hatakeyama-ha=== The Hatakeyama line (which has no current headmaster) uses the modern [[Dan (Rank)|dan]] system alongside the traditional ''menkyo'' system, issuing Ranks in both with shōden/chūden/okuden gradations to create equivalent Ranks from the ''menkyo'' system. ====Ranks==== *1st dan – kirikami shōden *2nd dan – kirikami chūden *3rd dan – mokuroku shōden *4th dan – mokuroku chūden *5th dan – menkyo mokuroku *6th dan – menkyo chūden *7th dan – menkyo okuden *8th dan – menkyo kaiden ===Sugawara-ha=== Sugawara Sogo Budo issues ''mokuroku'' and ''menkyo'' certifications. The highest level is menkyo kaiden. English-speaking members of Sugawara Budo refer to the Rank of menkyo as a "''kyōshi'' license" or ''menkyo kyōshi''. ===Noda-ha=== Noda Shinzan (1848–1917) began training in Katori Shintō-ryū in 1853 under Yamada Naomune, a retainer of the Date clan. Many Date retainers had trained in Katori Shintō-ryū and developed their own distinctive style. After Naomune's death in 1912, Shinzan enrolled his grandson, Seizan, under Yamaguchi Kumajiro, the most senior mainline shihan at the time. Following the deaths of Yamaguchi and his grandfather, Noda Seizan continued to train under Motomiya Toranosuke. After Motomiya's passing, Noda studied with Hayashi Yazaemon who began reforming and standardizing the mainline curriculum. Finding his style incompatible with Hayashi's, Noda Seizan quietly withdrew to private teaching, referring to his branch as Noda-ha Katori Shintō-ryū. Noda-ha preserves some kata that have been lost in the mainline. ===Ichigidō=== Shiigi Munenori began training in Katori Shintō-ryū under his father, who had trained with Motomiya Toranosuke, before formally training at Ōtake Risuke's dojo for over thirty years. He incorporates Katori Shintō-ryū as part of the curriculum of his Ichigidō organization and maintains a positive relationship with the Iizasa family. He writes "Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū" as "天真正伝香取神刀流" using '刀' (sword) in place of '道' (way). ===Yoseikan Budo=== Mochizuki Minoru, a judoka from the Kodokan who trained alongside Sugino Yoshio, has incorporated Katori Shintō-ryū into the curriculum of Yoseikan Budo. His son, Mochizuki Hiro, now heads Yoseikan. ==Notable practitioners== *[[:en:Yamazaki Susumu|Yamazaki Susumu]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=notes}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Further reading== * Amdur, Ellis (2002). ''Old School: Essays on Japanese Martial Traditions'', Edgework, p. 21–45 * Draeger, Donn F. ''The Martial Arts and Ways of Japan'' series, 3 volumes. * [[:en:Karl Friday|Friday, Karl F.]] (1997). ''Legacies of the Sword, the Kashima-Shinryu and Samurai Martial Culture'', University of Hawaii Press, p. 26 & 93, {{ISBN|0-8248-1847-4}} * Hall, David Avalon. ''Marishiten: Buddhism and the warrior Goddess'', Ph.D. dissertation, Ann Arbor: University microfilms, p. 274–292. * Hurst III, G. Cameron (1998). ''Armed Martial Arts of Japan, Swordsmanship and Archery'', Yale University Press, p. 46–49 & 58, {{ISBN|0-300-04967-6}} * Mol, Serge (2001). ''Classical Fighting Arts of Japan, A Complete Guide to Koryu JuJutsu'', Kodansha International, p. 43 & 151, {{ISBN|4-7700-2619-6}} * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 1'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-378-8}} (Original Japanese title for all three volumes in this series is ''Mukei Bunkazai Katori Shinto-ryu'') * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 2'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-405-9}} * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 3'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-406-7}} * Ōtake, Risuke (2016). ''Strategy and the Art of Peace: Tenshinshō-den Katori Shintō-ryū'' (English Edition). Nippon Budokan Foundation. {{ISBN|9784583109848}} ([http://iss.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I027049263-00 description page]) * Ratti, Oscar & Westbrook, Adele (1973). ''Secrets of the Samurai, A Survey of the Martial Arts of Feudal Japan'', Charles E. Tuttle Co. {{ISBN|0-8048-0917-8}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (1997). ''Koryu Bujutsu, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 1, {{ISBN|1-890536-04-0}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (1999). ''Sword & Spirit, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 2, p. 67–69. {{ISBN|1-890536-05-9}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (2002). ''Keiko Shokon, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 3, {{ISBN|1-890536-06-7}} * Sugino, Yoshio & Ito, Kikue (1977). ''Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu Budo Kyohan'' (A Textbook of Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu Martial Training; originally published in 1941). * [[:en:Gordon Warner|Warner, Gordon]] & Draeger, Donn F. (1982). ''Japanese Swordsmanship: Technique And Practice'', {{ISBN|0-8348-0236-8}} ==External links== {{Portal|Japan|Martial Arts}} {{Commons Category}} *[https://shinbukan-katorishintoryu.org/ Shinbukan Dojo Official Website] *[http://katorishintoryu-uk.com/ Katori Shinto-ryu United Kingdom] *[http://katorishintoryu-usa.org/ Katori Shinto-ryu United States] *[http://katori-shinto-ryu.org/ Katori Shinto-ryu Kyōsō Shibu Official Website] *[http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/main/ Katori Shinto-ryu Soke Official Website] {{navbox koryu}} {{Authority control}} ‎]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese martial arts]] }} [[sn:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[en:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ast:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]] [[ca:Katori Shinto-ryu]] {{Commons category|Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū}} [[cs:Tenšin šóden katori]] [[da:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]] [[de:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[es:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[fi:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]] [[fr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[he:טנשין שודן קאטורי שינטו ריו]] [[hu:Tensin sóden katori sintó-rjú]] [[id:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[it:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[ja:天真正伝香取神道流]] [[nl:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu]] [[no:Tenshinshoden Katori Shinto Ryu]] [[pl:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[pt:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]] [[ru:Тэнсин Сёдэн Катори Синто-рю]] [[sv:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[tr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[uk:Тенсин Седен Каторі Синто-рю]] [[vi:Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[zh:天真正傳香取神道流]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q112025]] Wikidata name: Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese martial art}} {{Use dmy dates|date=February 2020}} {{infobox koryu | school = Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū
(天真正伝香取神道流) | image = Kenjyutsu-2.jpg | imagecaption = Meiji Shrine festival special dedication | founder = [[:en:Iizasa Ienao|Iizasa Chōisai Ienao]] (飯篠 長威斉 家直, c.1387–c.1488) | date founded = c.1447 | period founded = Middle [[Muromachi period]] (1336–1573) | location = [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]] | headmaster = Yasusada Iizasa (飯篠 修理亮 快貞 ''Iizasa Shūri-no-Suke Yasusada'', born c.20th century) | headquarters = [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]] | art1 = ''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]'' | description1 = Sword art | art2 = ''[[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]'' | description2 = Art of drawing the sword | art3 = ''[[Ryōtōjutsu]]'' | description3 = Art of [[dual-wield]]ing swords (usu. one long one short) | art4 = ''[[:en:Bōjutsu|Bōjutsu]]'' | description4 = Staff art | art5 = ''[[:en:Naginatajutsu|Naginatajutsu]]'' | description5 = [[:en:Glaive|Glaive]] art | art6 = ''[[:en:Sōjutsu|Sōjutsu]]'' | description6 = Spear art | art7 = ''[[:en:Shurikenjutsu|Shurikenjutsu]]'' | description7 = [[Dart (missile)|Dart]] throwing art | art8 = [[:en:Jujutsu|Jujutsu]] | description8 = [[Hybrid martial arts|Hybrid]] art, unarmed or with minor weapons | ancestors = Katori no Ken, Kashima no Ken | assimilated = | descendants = {{hlist| [[Nakamura-ryū]] | [[Meifu Shinkage ryu|Meifu Shinkage-ryū]] | [[Shintō Musō-ryū]] | [[Yagyū Shinkage-ryū]] }} }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū'''|天真正伝香取神道流}}Written as 天眞正傳香取神道流 before adoption (1946) of [[:en:Tōyō kanji|Tōyō kanji]]. is one of the oldest extant [[:en:Japanese martial arts|Japanese martial arts]] and an exemplar of ''[[:en:bujutsu|bujutsu]]''. It was founded by [[:en:Iizasa Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]], who lived near [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] ([[:en:Sawara, Chiba|Sawara City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]) at the time. The ''[[:en:ryu (school)|ryū]]'' is purported to have been founded in 1447, but some scholars state that it was about 1480.Watatani, Kiyoshi (1967). Zusetsu Kobudōshi, TokyoThe year 1387 is given as Iizasa's birth year in ''Deity and the Sword'', Vol 1 pp. 16–17. Watatani (1967) speculates that 1417–1420 is correct. ==History== ===Foundation=== [[File:Katori-shinto-ryu,doujyou-training-hall,katori-city,japan.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Katori Shinto Ryu Dojo training hall in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori]] city, [[:en:Japan|Japan]]]] [[File:Iizasa_Chōisai_Ienao.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Iizasa Chōisai Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]] founded Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū in 1447]] [[:en:Iizasa Chōisai Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]] (飯篠 長威斎 家直 ''Iizasa Chōi-sai Ienao'', {{Circa}} 1387 – c. 1488) was a respected [[:en:Sōjutsu|spearman]] and [[:en:swordsman|swordsman]] whose ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' was deposed, which encouraged him to relinquish control of his household to conduct purification rituals and study martial arts in isolation.Amdur, Ellis (2002). ''Old School: Essays on Japanese Martial Traditions'', Edgework, p. 21–45 Iizasa was born in the village of Iizasa in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]. When he was young, he moved to the vicinity of the famous [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]], northeast of Tokyo in modern-day [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The shrine's ''[[kami]]'', [[Futsunushi]] ({{nihongo| 経津主神 |Futsunushi no kami}}) is revered as a spirit of swordsmanship and martial arts. ===Headmasters=== In 1896, the 18th ''[[:en:Sōke|Sōke]]'' died without a male heir. ''[[:en:Shihan|Shihan]]'' Yamaguchi Eikan governed the school until his death on 14 March 1917. Until Iizasa Kinjiro married into the Iizasa household, the school was headed in order by nine instructors—Tamai Kisaburo, Shiina Ichizo, Ito Tanekichi, Kuboki Sazaemon, Isobe Kohei, Motomiya (Hongu) Toranosuke, Hayashi Yazaemon, Kamagata Minosuke, and the main instructor Yamaguchi.Sugino, Yoshio (2016). ''Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu: Budo Kyohan'', Ulf Rott ===Recent history=== [[File:Risuke_Ōtake.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Risuke Otake|Risuke Otake]] (Pic. M. Stokhof 2007)]]On 6 March 1960, the school received the first ever "Intangible Cultural Asset" designation given to a martial art by the Japanese government, naming Hayashi Yazaemon, [[:en:Risuke Otake|Ōtake Risuke]], and Iizasa Yasusada as its guardians. The Iizasa family dojo was also designated a Cultural Asset. The designation of Cultural Asset status shifted to the Chiba Prefectural Government in 1985 and the art was recertified, again naming Ōtake Risuke and Iizasa Yasusada as guardians.Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition''. Koryu Books. p 22-23. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-21-3}} Ōtake Nobutoshi and Kyōsō Shigetoshi were also certified as guardians on 30 March 2004.{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p211-005.html|title = 武術 天真正伝香取神道流}} Iizasa Yasusada has publicly recognised the Otake and Sugino dojo. Both dojo represent the school annually at the Meiji Jingu embu. Kyōsō Shigetoshi was appointed shihan in September 2017,{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/instruction/|title=Instruction – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto Ryu}} and Ōtake Nobutoshi was issued hamon (excommunicated) on 9 December 2018.{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/important-message/|title=Important Message – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto Ryu}} However, both Otake Risuke, Otake Nobutoshi and Kyōsō Shigetoshi are still recognised as guardians of the school, by the Chiba Prefecture Board of Cultural Affairs.{{Cite web |date=20 April 2023 |title=天真正伝香取神道流 神武館 | Tenshinshō-den Katori Shintō-ryū Shinbukan Dōjō | 千葉県無形文化財 | 大竹利典 | Ōtake Risuke | 大竹信利 | Ōtake Nobutoshi - Introduction |url=https://shinbukan-katorishintoryu.org }} ===Gallery=== Kenjyutsu-2.jpg|Meiji Shrine annual festival dedication Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) Kenjyutsu-2a.jpg|Same as left, Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) Kenjyutsu-2b.jpg|Same as left, Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) == Curriculum == The Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū is a comprehensive martial system. Unlike modern martial arts such as [[:en:Kendo|Kendo]] or ''[[:en:Iaido|Iaido]]'', which concentrate on one specific area of training, a broad range of martial skills is studied.Skoss, Diane (editor) (1999). ''Sword & Spirit, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 2, p. 67–69. {{ISBN|1-890536-05-9}} The main emphasis of the school is ''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]''. A wide range of other weapons are taught as part of the curriculum, but the sword remains the central weapon. The primary curriculum includes:Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition''. Koryu Books. p 61. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-21-3}} {| border="1" cellpadding="2" cellspacing="0" ! colspan = "2" style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Arts''' ! style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Forms''' |- | rowspan = "8" style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Sword arts'''
(''tachijutsu'') | rowspan = "3" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Sword combat'''
(''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Sword''|表之太刀|Omote no Tachi}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of the Sword''|五行之太刀|Gogyō no Tachi}} |- | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Seven Essential Articles of the Sword''|極意七条之太刀|Gokui Shichijo no Tachi}} |- | rowspan = "3" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Sword drawing'''
(''[[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of Sword-Drawing''|表之居合|Omote-no Iai}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Standing Sword-Drawing''|立合抜刀術|Tachiai [[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Sword Drawing''|極意之居合|Gokui no Iai}} |- | style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Two swords'''
(両刀術 ''ryōtōjutsu'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Two Swords''|両刀|Ryōtō}} |- | style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Short sword'''
(''[[:en:Kodachi|Kodachi]]'') | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of the Short Sword''|極意之小太刀|Gokui no Kodachi}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Staff art'''
(''[[:en:Bōjutsu|Bōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Staff''|表之棒|Omote no Bō}} |- | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of the Staff''|五行之棒|Gogyō no Bō}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''[[:en:Glaive|Glaive]] art'''
(''[[:en:Naginatajutsu|Naginatajutsu]]'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Glaive''|表之長刀|Omote no Naginata}} |- | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Seven Essential Articles of the Glaive''|極意七条之長刀|Gokui Shichijo no Naginata}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Spear art'''
(''[[:en:Sōjutsu|Sōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Spear''|表之槍|Omote no Yari}} |- | 2 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Secret forms''||Hiden no Yari}} |- | rowspan = "3" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Spike-throwing'''
(''[[:en:Shurikenjutsu|Shurikenjutsu]]'') | 7 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of Spike Throwing''|表之手裏剣|Omote no Shuriken}} |- | 8 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of Spike Throwing''|五行之手裏剣|Gogyō no Shuriken}} |- | 9 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Spike Throwing''|極意之手裏剣|Gokui no Shuriken}} |- | colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''[[:en:Jujutsu|Jujutsu]]''' | 36 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Jujutsu''|極意之柔術|Gokui no Jūjutsu}} |} The Gogyo and Gokui kata are only taught to advanced practitioners after years of fundamental practice. Other, more advanced areas of study of the school include: *''Ninjutsu'' (intelligence gathering and analysis) *''Chikujojutsu'' (field fortification art) *''Gunbai-Heihō'' (strategy and tactics) *''Tenmon Chirigaku'' ([[:en:Chinese astrology|Chinese astrology]] and [[:en:Geomancy|geomantic divination]]) *''In-Yo kigaku'' (philosophical and mystical aspects derived from [[:en:Mikkyō|Mikkyō]], specifically [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]]) ==Keppan== Historically, before beginning any training in Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū, every prospective pupil had to swear an oath of allegiance to the school. The method was to make ''keppan'' (blood oath) in support of the following ''kisho'' or ''kishomon'' (pledge). This oath was a written one with the prospective member being required to sign his name in his own blood. The applicant would prick or cut a finger or sometimes the inner arm and with the blood drawn, sign the following pledge: Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition'', Koryu Books. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-20-6}} (A total revamp of ''The Deity and the Sword'', combining all 3 volumes into one re-translated and edited book with additional material. However, with far fewer photographs per kata as compared to the original 3 volumes. Similarly, all the original English explanatory notes and guidelines relating to each of the kata have been removed.)
On becoming a member of the Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū which has been transmitted by the Great Deity of the Katori Shrine, I herewith affirm my pledge that: #I will not have the impertinence to discuss or demonstrate details of the ''ryū'' to either non-members or members, even if they are relatives; #I will not engage in altercations or misuse the art against others; #I will never engage in any kind of gambling nor frequent disreputable places. #I will not cross swords with any followers of other martial traditions without authorization. I hereby pledge to firmly adhere to each of the above articles. Should I break any of these articles I will submit to the punishment of the Great Deity of Katori and the Great Deity Marishiten. Herewith I solemnly swear and affix my blood seal to this oath to these Great Deities.
==Branches== Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū is currently represented by the Kyōsō Shibu led by Kyōsō Shigetoshi, Ōtake Risuke's younger son. The honbu dojo is located at Iizasa Yasusada's home near the Katori shrine and is used by branches in good standing on special occasions. The Kyōsō Shibu regularly trains out of the Shisui Town Community Plaza and the Matsuyamashita Koen Sports Gymnasium in Inzai City.{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shinto-ryu.org/en/dojo/|title = Dojo – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto-ryu Kyoso Shibu}} Several branches have existed alongside or split from the mainline with varying ties to the Iizasa family, such as Noda, Yoseikan, Shinbukan, Sugino, Hatakeyama, Sugawara. ==Ranking and appointed positions== The different branches recognize different levels of Ranks and appointments. The traditional Ranks are a variant of the ''[[:en:menkyo|menkyo]]'' system.{{Cite web|url=http://judoinfo.com/Ranks/|title = Ranking Systems in Modern Japanese Martial Arts: Modern vs. Classical | Judo Info}} ===Ōtake=== ====Ranks==== * Mokuroku (目録, "catalog") * Menkyo (免許, "license, certificate") * Gokui Kaiden (極意皆伝, "deepest transmission") ====Appointments==== * Shidōsha (指導者, "mentor, coach"; given as license to teach outside of Shinbukan dojo) * Shihan (師範, 'instructor'; head-teacher) ===Sugino Dojo=== The Sugino line uses the modern [[Dan (Rank)|dan]] system, out of respect for Yoshio Sugino's (10th dan) judo teacher Kanō Jigorō, who was a pioneer of the dan-i Ranking system.{{Cite web|url=http://www.katsujinken.nl/2012/04/02/het-gradensysteem/|title = About the Katori Shinto ryu grading system | Katsujinken}} Sugino dojo members also receive densho/makimono. ===Hatakeyama-ha=== The Hatakeyama line (which has no current headmaster) uses the modern [[Dan (Rank)|dan]] system alongside the traditional ''menkyo'' system, issuing Ranks in both with shōden/chūden/okuden gradations to create equivalent Ranks from the ''menkyo'' system. ====Ranks==== *1st dan – kirikami shōden *2nd dan – kirikami chūden *3rd dan – mokuroku shōden *4th dan – mokuroku chūden *5th dan – menkyo mokuroku *6th dan – menkyo chūden *7th dan – menkyo okuden *8th dan – menkyo kaiden ===Sugawara-ha=== Sugawara Sogo Budo issues ''mokuroku'' and ''menkyo'' certifications. The highest level is menkyo kaiden. English-speaking members of Sugawara Budo refer to the Rank of menkyo as a "''kyōshi'' license" or ''menkyo kyōshi''. ===Noda-ha=== Noda Shinzan (1848–1917) began training in Katori Shintō-ryū in 1853 under Yamada Naomune, a retainer of the Date clan. Many Date retainers had trained in Katori Shintō-ryū and developed their own distinctive style. After Naomune's death in 1912, Shinzan enrolled his grandson, Seizan, under Yamaguchi Kumajiro, the most senior mainline shihan at the time. Following the deaths of Yamaguchi and his grandfather, Noda Seizan continued to train under Motomiya Toranosuke. After Motomiya's passing, Noda studied with Hayashi Yazaemon who began reforming and standardizing the mainline curriculum. Finding his style incompatible with Hayashi's, Noda Seizan quietly withdrew to private teaching, referring to his branch as Noda-ha Katori Shintō-ryū. Noda-ha preserves some kata that have been lost in the mainline. ===Ichigidō=== Shiigi Munenori began training in Katori Shintō-ryū under his father, who had trained with Motomiya Toranosuke, before formally training at Ōtake Risuke's dojo for over thirty years. He incorporates Katori Shintō-ryū as part of the curriculum of his Ichigidō organization and maintains a positive relationship with the Iizasa family. He writes "Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū" as "天真正伝香取神刀流" using '刀' (sword) in place of '道' (way). ===Yoseikan Budo=== Mochizuki Minoru, a judoka from the Kodokan who trained alongside Sugino Yoshio, has incorporated Katori Shintō-ryū into the curriculum of Yoseikan Budo. His son, Mochizuki Hiro, now heads Yoseikan. ==Notable practitioners== *[[:en:Yamazaki Susumu|Yamazaki Susumu]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=notes}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Further reading== * Amdur, Ellis (2002). ''Old School: Essays on Japanese Martial Traditions'', Edgework, p. 21–45 * Draeger, Donn F. ''The Martial Arts and Ways of Japan'' series, 3 volumes. * [[:en:Karl Friday|Friday, Karl F.]] (1997). ''Legacies of the Sword, the Kashima-Shinryu and Samurai Martial Culture'', University of Hawaii Press, p. 26 & 93, {{ISBN|0-8248-1847-4}} * Hall, David Avalon. ''Marishiten: Buddhism and the warrior Goddess'', Ph.D. dissertation, Ann Arbor: University microfilms, p. 274–292. * Hurst III, G. Cameron (1998). ''Armed Martial Arts of Japan, Swordsmanship and Archery'', Yale University Press, p. 46–49 & 58, {{ISBN|0-300-04967-6}} * Mol, Serge (2001). ''Classical Fighting Arts of Japan, A Complete Guide to Koryu JuJutsu'', Kodansha International, p. 43 & 151, {{ISBN|4-7700-2619-6}} * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 1'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-378-8}} (Original Japanese title for all three volumes in this series is ''Mukei Bunkazai Katori Shinto-ryu'') * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 2'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-405-9}} * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 3'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-406-7}} * Ōtake, Risuke (2016). ''Strategy and the Art of Peace: Tenshinshō-den Katori Shintō-ryū'' (English Edition). Nippon Budokan Foundation. {{ISBN|9784583109848}} ([http://iss.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I027049263-00 description page]) * Ratti, Oscar & Westbrook, Adele (1973). ''Secrets of the Samurai, A Survey of the Martial Arts of Feudal Japan'', Charles E. Tuttle Co. {{ISBN|0-8048-0917-8}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (1997). ''Koryu Bujutsu, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 1, {{ISBN|1-890536-04-0}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (1999). ''Sword & Spirit, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 2, p. 67–69. {{ISBN|1-890536-05-9}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (2002). ''Keiko Shokon, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 3, {{ISBN|1-890536-06-7}} * Sugino, Yoshio & Ito, Kikue (1977). ''Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu Budo Kyohan'' (A Textbook of Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu Martial Training; originally published in 1941). * [[:en:Gordon Warner|Warner, Gordon]] & Draeger, Donn F. (1982). ''Japanese Swordsmanship: Technique And Practice'', {{ISBN|0-8348-0236-8}} ==External links== {{Portal|Japan|Martial Arts}} {{Commons Category}} *[https://shinbukan-katorishintoryu.org/ Shinbukan Dojo Official Website] *[http://katorishintoryu-uk.com/ Katori Shinto-ryu United Kingdom] *[http://katorishintoryu-usa.org/ Katori Shinto-ryu United States] *[http://katori-shinto-ryu.org/ Katori Shinto-ryu Kyōsō Shibu Official Website] *[http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/main/ Katori Shinto-ryu Soke Official Website] {{navbox koryu}} {{Authority control}} ‎]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese martial arts]] }} [[sn:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[en:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ast:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]] [[ca:Katori Shinto-ryu]] {{Commons category|Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū}} [[cs:Tenšin šóden katori]] [[da:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]] [[de:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[es:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[fi:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]] [[fr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[he:טנשין שודן קאטורי שינטו ריו]] [[hu:Tensin sóden katori sintó-rjú]] [[id:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[it:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[ja:天真正伝香取神道流]] [[nl:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu]] [[no:Tenshinshoden Katori Shinto Ryu]] [[pl:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[pt:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]] [[ru:Тэнсин Сёдэн Катори Синто-рю]] [[sv:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[tr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[uk:Тенсин Седен Каторі Синто-рю]] [[vi:Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[zh:天真正傳香取神道流]] {{wikidata link|Q112025}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q112025]] Wikidata name: Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese martial art}} {{Use dmy dates|date=February 2020}} {{infobox koryu | school = Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū
(天真正伝香取神道流) | image = Kenjyutsu-2.jpg | imagecaption = Meiji Shrine festival special dedication | founder = [[:en:Iizasa Ienao|Iizasa Chōisai Ienao]] (飯篠 長威斉 家直, c.1387–c.1488) | date founded = c.1447 | period founded = Middle [[Muromachi period]] (1336–1573) | location = [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]] | headmaster = Yasusada Iizasa (飯篠 修理亮 快貞 ''Iizasa Shūri-no-Suke Yasusada'', born c.20th century) | headquarters = [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]] | art1 = ''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]'' | description1 = Sword art | art2 = ''[[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]'' | description2 = Art of drawing the sword | art3 = ''[[Ryōtōjutsu]]'' | description3 = Art of [[dual-wield]]ing swords (usu. one long one short) | art4 = ''[[:en:Bōjutsu|Bōjutsu]]'' | description4 = Staff art | art5 = ''[[:en:Naginatajutsu|Naginatajutsu]]'' | description5 = [[:en:Glaive|Glaive]] art | art6 = ''[[:en:Sōjutsu|Sōjutsu]]'' | description6 = Spear art | art7 = ''[[:en:Shurikenjutsu|Shurikenjutsu]]'' | description7 = [[Dart (missile)|Dart]] throwing art | art8 = [[:en:Jujutsu|Jujutsu]] | description8 = [[Hybrid martial arts|Hybrid]] art, unarmed or with minor weapons | ancestors = Katori no Ken, Kashima no Ken | assimilated = | descendants = {{hlist| [[Nakamura-ryū]] | [[Meifu Shinkage ryu|Meifu Shinkage-ryū]] | [[Shintō Musō-ryū]] | [[Yagyū Shinkage-ryū]] }} }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū'''|天真正伝香取神道流}}Written as 天眞正傳香取神道流 before adoption (1946) of [[:en:Tōyō kanji|Tōyō kanji]]. is one of the oldest extant [[:en:Japanese martial arts|Japanese martial arts]] and an exemplar of ''[[:en:bujutsu|bujutsu]]''. It was founded by [[:en:Iizasa Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]], who lived near [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] ([[:en:Sawara, Chiba|Sawara City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]) at the time. The ''[[:en:ryu (school)|ryū]]'' is purported to have been founded in 1447, but some scholars state that it was about 1480.Watatani, Kiyoshi (1967). Zusetsu Kobudōshi, TokyoThe year 1387 is given as Iizasa's birth year in ''Deity and the Sword'', Vol 1 pp. 16–17. Watatani (1967) speculates that 1417–1420 is correct. ==History== ===Foundation=== [[File:Katori-shinto-ryu,doujyou-training-hall,katori-city,japan.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Katori Shinto Ryu Dojo training hall in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori]] city, [[:en:Japan|Japan]]]] [[File:Iizasa_Chōisai_Ienao.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Iizasa Chōisai Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]] founded Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū in 1447]] [[:en:Iizasa Chōisai Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]] (飯篠 長威斎 家直 ''Iizasa Chōi-sai Ienao'', {{Circa}} 1387 – c. 1488) was a respected [[:en:Sōjutsu|spearman]] and [[:en:swordsman|swordsman]] whose ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' was deposed, which encouraged him to relinquish control of his household to conduct purification rituals and study martial arts in isolation.Amdur, Ellis (2002). ''Old School: Essays on Japanese Martial Traditions'', Edgework, p. 21–45 Iizasa was born in the village of Iizasa in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]. When he was young, he moved to the vicinity of the famous [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]], northeast of Tokyo in modern-day [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The shrine's ''[[kami]]'', [[Futsunushi]] ({{nihongo| 経津主神 |Futsunushi no kami}}) is revered as a spirit of swordsmanship and martial arts. ===Headmasters=== In 1896, the 18th ''[[:en:Sōke|Sōke]]'' died without a male heir. ''[[:en:Shihan|Shihan]]'' Yamaguchi Eikan governed the school until his death on 14 March 1917. Until Iizasa Kinjiro married into the Iizasa household, the school was headed in order by nine instructors—Tamai Kisaburo, Shiina Ichizo, Ito Tanekichi, Kuboki Sazaemon, Isobe Kohei, Motomiya (Hongu) Toranosuke, Hayashi Yazaemon, Kamagata Minosuke, and the main instructor Yamaguchi.Sugino, Yoshio (2016). ''Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu: Budo Kyohan'', Ulf Rott ===Recent history=== [[File:Risuke_Ōtake.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Risuke Otake|Risuke Otake]] (Pic. M. Stokhof 2007)]]On 6 March 1960, the school received the first ever "Intangible Cultural Asset" designation given to a martial art by the Japanese government, naming Hayashi Yazaemon, [[:en:Risuke Otake|Ōtake Risuke]], and Iizasa Yasusada as its guardians. The Iizasa family dojo was also designated a Cultural Asset. The designation of Cultural Asset status shifted to the Chiba Prefectural Government in 1985 and the art was recertified, again naming Ōtake Risuke and Iizasa Yasusada as guardians.Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition''. Koryu Books. p 22-23. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-21-3}} Ōtake Nobutoshi and Kyōsō Shigetoshi were also certified as guardians on 30 March 2004.{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p211-005.html|title = 武術 天真正伝香取神道流}} Iizasa Yasusada has publicly recognised the Otake and Sugino dojo. Both dojo represent the school annually at the Meiji Jingu embu. Kyōsō Shigetoshi was appointed shihan in September 2017,{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/instruction/|title=Instruction – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto Ryu}} and Ōtake Nobutoshi was issued hamon (excommunicated) on 9 December 2018.{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/important-message/|title=Important Message – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto Ryu}} However, both Otake Risuke, Otake Nobutoshi and Kyōsō Shigetoshi are still recognised as guardians of the school, by the Chiba Prefecture Board of Cultural Affairs.{{Cite web |date=20 April 2023 |title=天真正伝香取神道流 神武館 | Tenshinshō-den Katori Shintō-ryū Shinbukan Dōjō | 千葉県無形文化財 | 大竹利典 | Ōtake Risuke | 大竹信利 | Ōtake Nobutoshi - Introduction |url=https://shinbukan-katorishintoryu.org }} ===Gallery=== Kenjyutsu-2.jpg|Meiji Shrine annual festival dedication Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) Kenjyutsu-2a.jpg|Same as left, Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) Kenjyutsu-2b.jpg|Same as left, Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) == Curriculum == The Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū is a comprehensive martial system. Unlike modern martial arts such as [[:en:Kendo|Kendo]] or ''[[:en:Iaido|Iaido]]'', which concentrate on one specific area of training, a broad range of martial skills is studied.Skoss, Diane (editor) (1999). ''Sword & Spirit, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 2, p. 67–69. {{ISBN|1-890536-05-9}} The main emphasis of the school is ''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]''. A wide range of other weapons are taught as part of the curriculum, but the sword remains the central weapon. The primary curriculum includes:Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition''. Koryu Books. p 61. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-21-3}} {| border="1" cellpadding="2" cellspacing="0" ! colspan = "2" style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Arts''' ! style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Forms''' |- | rowspan = "8" style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Sword arts'''
(''tachijutsu'') | rowspan = "3" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Sword combat'''
(''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Sword''|表之太刀|Omote no Tachi}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of the Sword''|五行之太刀|Gogyō no Tachi}} |- | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Seven Essential Articles of the Sword''|極意七条之太刀|Gokui Shichijo no Tachi}} |- | rowspan = "3" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Sword drawing'''
(''[[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of Sword-Drawing''|表之居合|Omote-no Iai}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Standing Sword-Drawing''|立合抜刀術|Tachiai [[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Sword Drawing''|極意之居合|Gokui no Iai}} |- | style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Two swords'''
(両刀術 ''ryōtōjutsu'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Two Swords''|両刀|Ryōtō}} |- | style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Short sword'''
(''[[:en:Kodachi|Kodachi]]'') | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of the Short Sword''|極意之小太刀|Gokui no Kodachi}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Staff art'''
(''[[:en:Bōjutsu|Bōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Staff''|表之棒|Omote no Bō}} |- | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of the Staff''|五行之棒|Gogyō no Bō}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''[[:en:Glaive|Glaive]] art'''
(''[[:en:Naginatajutsu|Naginatajutsu]]'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Glaive''|表之長刀|Omote no Naginata}} |- | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Seven Essential Articles of the Glaive''|極意七条之長刀|Gokui Shichijo no Naginata}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Spear art'''
(''[[:en:Sōjutsu|Sōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Spear''|表之槍|Omote no Yari}} |- | 2 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Secret forms''||Hiden no Yari}} |- | rowspan = "3" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Spike-throwing'''
(''[[:en:Shurikenjutsu|Shurikenjutsu]]'') | 7 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of Spike Throwing''|表之手裏剣|Omote no Shuriken}} |- | 8 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of Spike Throwing''|五行之手裏剣|Gogyō no Shuriken}} |- | 9 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Spike Throwing''|極意之手裏剣|Gokui no Shuriken}} |- | colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''[[:en:Jujutsu|Jujutsu]]''' | 36 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Jujutsu''|極意之柔術|Gokui no Jūjutsu}} |} The Gogyo and Gokui kata are only taught to advanced practitioners after years of fundamental practice. Other, more advanced areas of study of the school include: *''Ninjutsu'' (intelligence gathering and analysis) *''Chikujojutsu'' (field fortification art) *''Gunbai-Heihō'' (strategy and tactics) *''Tenmon Chirigaku'' ([[:en:Chinese astrology|Chinese astrology]] and [[:en:Geomancy|geomantic divination]]) *''In-Yo kigaku'' (philosophical and mystical aspects derived from [[:en:Mikkyō|Mikkyō]], specifically [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]]) ==Keppan== Historically, before beginning any training in Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū, every prospective pupil had to swear an oath of allegiance to the school. The method was to make ''keppan'' (blood oath) in support of the following ''kisho'' or ''kishomon'' (pledge). This oath was a written one with the prospective member being required to sign his name in his own blood. The applicant would prick or cut a finger or sometimes the inner arm and with the blood drawn, sign the following pledge: Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition'', Koryu Books. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-20-6}} (A total revamp of ''The Deity and the Sword'', combining all 3 volumes into one re-translated and edited book with additional material. However, with far fewer photographs per kata as compared to the original 3 volumes. Similarly, all the original English explanatory notes and guidelines relating to each of the kata have been removed.)
On becoming a member of the Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū which has been transmitted by the Great Deity of the Katori Shrine, I herewith affirm my pledge that: #I will not have the impertinence to discuss or demonstrate details of the ''ryū'' to either non-members or members, even if they are relatives; #I will not engage in altercations or misuse the art against others; #I will never engage in any kind of gambling nor frequent disreputable places. #I will not cross swords with any followers of other martial traditions without authorization. I hereby pledge to firmly adhere to each of the above articles. Should I break any of these articles I will submit to the punishment of the Great Deity of Katori and the Great Deity Marishiten. Herewith I solemnly swear and affix my blood seal to this oath to these Great Deities.
==Branches== Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū is currently represented by the Kyōsō Shibu led by Kyōsō Shigetoshi, Ōtake Risuke's younger son. The honbu dojo is located at Iizasa Yasusada's home near the Katori shrine and is used by branches in good standing on special occasions. The Kyōsō Shibu regularly trains out of the Shisui Town Community Plaza and the Matsuyamashita Koen Sports Gymnasium in Inzai City.{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shinto-ryu.org/en/dojo/|title = Dojo – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto-ryu Kyoso Shibu}} Several branches have existed alongside or split from the mainline with varying ties to the Iizasa family, such as Noda, Yoseikan, Shinbukan, Sugino, Hatakeyama, Sugawara. ==Ranking and appointed positions== The different branches recognize different levels of Ranks and appointments. The traditional Ranks are a variant of the ''[[:en:menkyo|menkyo]]'' system.{{Cite web|url=http://judoinfo.com/Ranks/|title = Ranking Systems in Modern Japanese Martial Arts: Modern vs. Classical | Judo Info}} ===Ōtake=== ====Ranks==== * Mokuroku (目録, "catalog") * Menkyo (免許, "license, certificate") * Gokui Kaiden (極意皆伝, "deepest transmission") ====Appointments==== * Shidōsha (指導者, "mentor, coach"; given as license to teach outside of Shinbukan dojo) * Shihan (師範, 'instructor'; head-teacher) ===Sugino Dojo=== The Sugino line uses the modern [[Dan (Rank)|dan]] system, out of respect for Yoshio Sugino's (10th dan) judo teacher Kanō Jigorō, who was a pioneer of the dan-i Ranking system.{{Cite web|url=http://www.katsujinken.nl/2012/04/02/het-gradensysteem/|title = About the Katori Shinto ryu grading system | Katsujinken}} Sugino dojo members also receive densho/makimono. ===Hatakeyama-ha=== The Hatakeyama line (which has no current headmaster) uses the modern [[Dan (Rank)|dan]] system alongside the traditional ''menkyo'' system, issuing Ranks in both with shōden/chūden/okuden gradations to create equivalent Ranks from the ''menkyo'' system. ====Ranks==== *1st dan – kirikami shōden *2nd dan – kirikami chūden *3rd dan – mokuroku shōden *4th dan – mokuroku chūden *5th dan – menkyo mokuroku *6th dan – menkyo chūden *7th dan – menkyo okuden *8th dan – menkyo kaiden ===Sugawara-ha=== Sugawara Sogo Budo issues ''mokuroku'' and ''menkyo'' certifications. The highest level is menkyo kaiden. English-speaking members of Sugawara Budo refer to the Rank of menkyo as a "''kyōshi'' license" or ''menkyo kyōshi''. ===Noda-ha=== Noda Shinzan (1848–1917) began training in Katori Shintō-ryū in 1853 under Yamada Naomune, a retainer of the Date clan. Many Date retainers had trained in Katori Shintō-ryū and developed their own distinctive style. After Naomune's death in 1912, Shinzan enrolled his grandson, Seizan, under Yamaguchi Kumajiro, the most senior mainline shihan at the time. Following the deaths of Yamaguchi and his grandfather, Noda Seizan continued to train under Motomiya Toranosuke. After Motomiya's passing, Noda studied with Hayashi Yazaemon who began reforming and standardizing the mainline curriculum. Finding his style incompatible with Hayashi's, Noda Seizan quietly withdrew to private teaching, referring to his branch as Noda-ha Katori Shintō-ryū. Noda-ha preserves some kata that have been lost in the mainline. ===Ichigidō=== Shiigi Munenori began training in Katori Shintō-ryū under his father, who had trained with Motomiya Toranosuke, before formally training at Ōtake Risuke's dojo for over thirty years. He incorporates Katori Shintō-ryū as part of the curriculum of his Ichigidō organization and maintains a positive relationship with the Iizasa family. He writes "Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū" as "天真正伝香取神刀流" using '刀' (sword) in place of '道' (way). ===Yoseikan Budo=== Mochizuki Minoru, a judoka from the Kodokan who trained alongside Sugino Yoshio, has incorporated Katori Shintō-ryū into the curriculum of Yoseikan Budo. His son, Mochizuki Hiro, now heads Yoseikan. ==Notable practitioners== *[[:en:Yamazaki Susumu|Yamazaki Susumu]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=notes}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Further reading== * Amdur, Ellis (2002). ''Old School: Essays on Japanese Martial Traditions'', Edgework, p. 21–45 * Draeger, Donn F. ''The Martial Arts and Ways of Japan'' series, 3 volumes. * [[:en:Karl Friday|Friday, Karl F.]] (1997). ''Legacies of the Sword, the Kashima-Shinryu and Samurai Martial Culture'', University of Hawaii Press, p. 26 & 93, {{ISBN|0-8248-1847-4}} * Hall, David Avalon. ''Marishiten: Buddhism and the warrior Goddess'', Ph.D. dissertation, Ann Arbor: University microfilms, p. 274–292. * Hurst III, G. Cameron (1998). ''Armed Martial Arts of Japan, Swordsmanship and Archery'', Yale University Press, p. 46–49 & 58, {{ISBN|0-300-04967-6}} * Mol, Serge (2001). ''Classical Fighting Arts of Japan, A Complete Guide to Koryu JuJutsu'', Kodansha International, p. 43 & 151, {{ISBN|4-7700-2619-6}} * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 1'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-378-8}} (Original Japanese title for all three volumes in this series is ''Mukei Bunkazai Katori Shinto-ryu'') * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 2'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-405-9}} * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 3'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-406-7}} * Ōtake, Risuke (2016). ''Strategy and the Art of Peace: Tenshinshō-den Katori Shintō-ryū'' (English Edition). Nippon Budokan Foundation. {{ISBN|9784583109848}} ([http://iss.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I027049263-00 description page]) * Ratti, Oscar & Westbrook, Adele (1973). ''Secrets of the Samurai, A Survey of the Martial Arts of Feudal Japan'', Charles E. Tuttle Co. {{ISBN|0-8048-0917-8}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (1997). ''Koryu Bujutsu, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 1, {{ISBN|1-890536-04-0}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (1999). ''Sword & Spirit, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 2, p. 67–69. {{ISBN|1-890536-05-9}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (2002). ''Keiko Shokon, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 3, {{ISBN|1-890536-06-7}} * Sugino, Yoshio & Ito, Kikue (1977). ''Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu Budo Kyohan'' (A Textbook of Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu Martial Training; originally published in 1941). * [[:en:Gordon Warner|Warner, Gordon]] & Draeger, Donn F. (1982). ''Japanese Swordsmanship: Technique And Practice'', {{ISBN|0-8348-0236-8}} ==External links== {{Portal|Japan|Martial Arts}} {{Commons Category}} *[https://shinbukan-katorishintoryu.org/ Shinbukan Dojo Official Website] *[http://katorishintoryu-uk.com/ Katori Shinto-ryu United Kingdom] *[http://katorishintoryu-usa.org/ Katori Shinto-ryu United States] *[http://katori-shinto-ryu.org/ Katori Shinto-ryu Kyōsō Shibu Official Website] *[http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/main/ Katori Shinto-ryu Soke Official Website] {{navbox koryu}} {{Authority control}} ‎]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese martial arts]] }} [[en:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ast:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]] [[ca:Katori Shinto-ryu]] {{Commons category|Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū}} [[cs:Tenšin šóden katori]] [[da:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]] [[de:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[es:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[fi:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]] [[fr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[he:טנשין שודן קאטורי שינטו ריו]] [[hu:Tensin sóden katori sintó-rjú]] [[id:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[it:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[ja:天真正伝香取神道流]] [[nl:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu]] [[no:Tenshinshoden Katori Shinto Ryu]] [[pl:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[pt:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]] [[ru:Тэнсин Сёдэн Катори Синто-рю]] [[sv:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[tr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[uk:Тенсин Седен Каторі Синто-рю]] [[vi:Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[zh:天真正傳香取神道流]] {{wikidata link|Q112025}}

[[ast:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]][[ca:Katori Shinto-ryu]][[commons:Category:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[cs:Tenšin šóden katori]][[da:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]][[de:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[en:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[es:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[fi:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]][[fr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[he:טנשין שודן קאטורי שינטו ריו]][[hu:Tensin sóden katori sintó-rjú]][[id:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[it:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[ja:天真正伝香取神道流]][[nl:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu]][[no:Tenshinshoden Katori Shinto Ryu]][[pl:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[pt:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]][[ru:Тэнсин Сёдэн Катори Синто-рю]][[sv:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[tr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]][[uk:Тенсин Седен Каторі Синто-рю]][[vi:Katori Shintō-ryū]][[zh:天真正傳香取神道流]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q112025]] Wikidata name: Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese martial art}} {{Use dmy dates|date=February 2020}} {{infobox koryu | school = Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū
(天真正伝香取神道流) | image = Kenjyutsu-2.jpg | imagecaption = Meiji Shrine festival special dedication | founder = [[:en:Iizasa Ienao|Iizasa Chōisai Ienao]] (飯篠 長威斉 家直, c.1387–c.1488) | date founded = c.1447 | period founded = Middle [[Muromachi period]] (1336–1573) | location = [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]] | headmaster = Yasusada Iizasa (飯篠 修理亮 快貞 ''Iizasa Shūri-no-Suke Yasusada'', born c.20th century) | headquarters = [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]] | art1 = ''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]'' | description1 = Sword art | art2 = ''[[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]'' | description2 = Art of drawing the sword | art3 = ''[[Ryōtōjutsu]]'' | description3 = Art of [[dual-wield]]ing swords (usu. one long one short) | art4 = ''[[:en:Bōjutsu|Bōjutsu]]'' | description4 = Staff art | art5 = ''[[:en:Naginatajutsu|Naginatajutsu]]'' | description5 = [[:en:Glaive|Glaive]] art | art6 = ''[[:en:Sōjutsu|Sōjutsu]]'' | description6 = Spear art | art7 = ''[[:en:Shurikenjutsu|Shurikenjutsu]]'' | description7 = [[Dart (missile)|Dart]] throwing art | art8 = [[:en:Jujutsu|Jujutsu]] | description8 = [[Hybrid martial arts|Hybrid]] art, unarmed or with minor weapons | ancestors = Katori no Ken, Kashima no Ken | assimilated = | descendants = {{hlist| [[Nakamura-ryū]] | [[Meifu Shinkage ryu|Meifu Shinkage-ryū]] | [[Shintō Musō-ryū]] | [[Yagyū Shinkage-ryū]] }} }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū'''|天真正伝香取神道流}}Written as 天眞正傳香取神道流 before adoption (1946) of [[:en:Tōyō kanji|Tōyō kanji]]. is one of the oldest extant [[:en:Japanese martial arts|Japanese martial arts]] and an exemplar of ''[[:en:bujutsu|bujutsu]]''. It was founded by [[:en:Iizasa Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]], who lived near [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] ([[:en:Sawara, Chiba|Sawara City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]) at the time. The ''[[:en:ryu (school)|ryū]]'' is purported to have been founded in 1447, but some scholars state that it was about 1480.Watatani, Kiyoshi (1967). Zusetsu Kobudōshi, TokyoThe year 1387 is given as Iizasa's birth year in ''Deity and the Sword'', Vol 1 pp. 16–17. Watatani (1967) speculates that 1417–1420 is correct. ==History== ===Foundation=== [[File:Katori-shinto-ryu,doujyou-training-hall,katori-city,japan.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Katori Shinto Ryu Dojo training hall in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori]] city, [[:en:Japan|Japan]]]] [[File:Iizasa_Chōisai_Ienao.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Iizasa Chōisai Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]] founded Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū in 1447]] [[:en:Iizasa Chōisai Ienao|Iizasa Ienao]] (飯篠 長威斎 家直 ''Iizasa Chōi-sai Ienao'', {{Circa}} 1387 – c. 1488) was a respected [[:en:Sōjutsu|spearman]] and [[:en:swordsman|swordsman]] whose ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' was deposed, which encouraged him to relinquish control of his household to conduct purification rituals and study martial arts in isolation.Amdur, Ellis (2002). ''Old School: Essays on Japanese Martial Traditions'', Edgework, p. 21–45 Iizasa was born in the village of Iizasa in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]. When he was young, he moved to the vicinity of the famous [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]], northeast of Tokyo in modern-day [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The shrine's ''[[kami]]'', [[Futsunushi]] ({{nihongo| 経津主神 |Futsunushi no kami}}) is revered as a spirit of swordsmanship and martial arts. ===Headmasters=== In 1896, the 18th ''[[:en:Sōke|Sōke]]'' died without a male heir. ''[[:en:Shihan|Shihan]]'' Yamaguchi Eikan governed the school until his death on 14 March 1917. Until Iizasa Kinjiro married into the Iizasa household, the school was headed in order by nine instructors—Tamai Kisaburo, Shiina Ichizo, Ito Tanekichi, Kuboki Sazaemon, Isobe Kohei, Motomiya (Hongu) Toranosuke, Hayashi Yazaemon, Kamagata Minosuke, and the main instructor Yamaguchi.Sugino, Yoshio (2016). ''Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu: Budo Kyohan'', Ulf Rott ===Recent history=== [[File:Risuke_Ōtake.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Risuke Otake|Risuke Otake]] (Pic. M. Stokhof 2007)]]On 6 March 1960, the school received the first ever "Intangible Cultural Asset" designation given to a martial art by the Japanese government, naming Hayashi Yazaemon, [[:en:Risuke Otake|Ōtake Risuke]], and Iizasa Yasusada as its guardians. The Iizasa family dojo was also designated a Cultural Asset. The designation of Cultural Asset status shifted to the Chiba Prefectural Government in 1985 and the art was recertified, again naming Ōtake Risuke and Iizasa Yasusada as guardians.Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition''. Koryu Books. p 22-23. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-21-3}} Ōtake Nobutoshi and Kyōsō Shigetoshi were also certified as guardians on 30 March 2004.{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p211-005.html|title = 武術 天真正伝香取神道流}} Iizasa Yasusada has publicly recognised the Otake and Sugino dojo. Both dojo represent the school annually at the Meiji Jingu embu. Kyōsō Shigetoshi was appointed shihan in September 2017,{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/instruction/|title=Instruction – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto Ryu}} and Ōtake Nobutoshi was issued hamon (excommunicated) on 9 December 2018.{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/important-message/|title=Important Message – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto Ryu}} However, both Otake Risuke, Otake Nobutoshi and Kyōsō Shigetoshi are still recognised as guardians of the school, by the Chiba Prefecture Board of Cultural Affairs.{{Cite web |date=20 April 2023 |title=天真正伝香取神道流 神武館 | Tenshinshō-den Katori Shintō-ryū Shinbukan Dōjō | 千葉県無形文化財 | 大竹利典 | Ōtake Risuke | 大竹信利 | Ōtake Nobutoshi - Introduction |url=https://shinbukan-katorishintoryu.org }} ===Gallery=== Kenjyutsu-2.jpg|Meiji Shrine annual festival dedication Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) Kenjyutsu-2a.jpg|Same as left, Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) Kenjyutsu-2b.jpg|Same as left, Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu (3 November 2011) == Curriculum == The Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū is a comprehensive martial system. Unlike modern martial arts such as [[:en:Kendo|Kendo]] or ''[[:en:Iaido|Iaido]]'', which concentrate on one specific area of training, a broad range of martial skills is studied.Skoss, Diane (editor) (1999). ''Sword & Spirit, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 2, p. 67–69. {{ISBN|1-890536-05-9}} The main emphasis of the school is ''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]''. A wide range of other weapons are taught as part of the curriculum, but the sword remains the central weapon. The primary curriculum includes:Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition''. Koryu Books. p 61. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-21-3}} {| border="1" cellpadding="2" cellspacing="0" ! colspan = "2" style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Arts''' ! style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Forms''' |- | rowspan = "8" style = "background:#ddf;" | '''Sword arts'''
(''tachijutsu'') | rowspan = "3" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Sword combat'''
(''[[:en:Kenjutsu|Kenjutsu]]'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Sword''|表之太刀|Omote no Tachi}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of the Sword''|五行之太刀|Gogyō no Tachi}} |- | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Seven Essential Articles of the Sword''|極意七条之太刀|Gokui Shichijo no Tachi}} |- | rowspan = "3" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Sword drawing'''
(''[[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of Sword-Drawing''|表之居合|Omote-no Iai}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Standing Sword-Drawing''|立合抜刀術|Tachiai [[:en:Battōjutsu|Battōjutsu]]}} |- | 5 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Sword Drawing''|極意之居合|Gokui no Iai}} |- | style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Two swords'''
(両刀術 ''ryōtōjutsu'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Two Swords''|両刀|Ryōtō}} |- | style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Short sword'''
(''[[:en:Kodachi|Kodachi]]'') | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of the Short Sword''|極意之小太刀|Gokui no Kodachi}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Staff art'''
(''[[:en:Bōjutsu|Bōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Staff''|表之棒|Omote no Bō}} |- | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of the Staff''|五行之棒|Gogyō no Bō}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''[[:en:Glaive|Glaive]] art'''
(''[[:en:Naginatajutsu|Naginatajutsu]]'') | 4 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Glaive''|表之長刀|Omote no Naginata}} |- | 3 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Seven Essential Articles of the Glaive''|極意七条之長刀|Gokui Shichijo no Naginata}} |- | rowspan = "2" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Spear art'''
(''[[:en:Sōjutsu|Sōjutsu]]'') | 6 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of the Spear''|表之槍|Omote no Yari}} |- | 2 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Secret forms''||Hiden no Yari}} |- | rowspan = "3" colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''Spike-throwing'''
(''[[:en:Shurikenjutsu|Shurikenjutsu]]'') | 7 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Basics of Spike Throwing''|表之手裏剣|Omote no Shuriken}} |- | 8 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Five Teachings of Spike Throwing''|五行之手裏剣|Gogyō no Shuriken}} |- | 9 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Spike Throwing''|極意之手裏剣|Gokui no Shuriken}} |- | colspan = "2" style = "background:#AAAEEE" | '''[[:en:Jujutsu|Jujutsu]]''' | 36 Forms: {{Nihongo|''Essentials of Jujutsu''|極意之柔術|Gokui no Jūjutsu}} |} The Gogyo and Gokui kata are only taught to advanced practitioners after years of fundamental practice. Other, more advanced areas of study of the school include: *''Ninjutsu'' (intelligence gathering and analysis) *''Chikujojutsu'' (field fortification art) *''Gunbai-Heihō'' (strategy and tactics) *''Tenmon Chirigaku'' ([[:en:Chinese astrology|Chinese astrology]] and [[:en:Geomancy|geomantic divination]]) *''In-Yo kigaku'' (philosophical and mystical aspects derived from [[:en:Mikkyō|Mikkyō]], specifically [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]]) ==Keppan== Historically, before beginning any training in Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū, every prospective pupil had to swear an oath of allegiance to the school. The method was to make ''keppan'' (blood oath) in support of the following ''kisho'' or ''kishomon'' (pledge). This oath was a written one with the prospective member being required to sign his name in his own blood. The applicant would prick or cut a finger or sometimes the inner arm and with the blood drawn, sign the following pledge: Ōtake, Risuke (2007). ''Katori Shinto-ryu: Warrior Tradition'', Koryu Books. {{ISBN|978-1-890536-20-6}} (A total revamp of ''The Deity and the Sword'', combining all 3 volumes into one re-translated and edited book with additional material. However, with far fewer photographs per kata as compared to the original 3 volumes. Similarly, all the original English explanatory notes and guidelines relating to each of the kata have been removed.)
On becoming a member of the Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū which has been transmitted by the Great Deity of the Katori Shrine, I herewith affirm my pledge that: #I will not have the impertinence to discuss or demonstrate details of the ''ryū'' to either non-members or members, even if they are relatives; #I will not engage in altercations or misuse the art against others; #I will never engage in any kind of gambling nor frequent disreputable places. #I will not cross swords with any followers of other martial traditions without authorization. I hereby pledge to firmly adhere to each of the above articles. Should I break any of these articles I will submit to the punishment of the Great Deity of Katori and the Great Deity Marishiten. Herewith I solemnly swear and affix my blood seal to this oath to these Great Deities.
==Branches== Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū is currently represented by the Kyōsō Shibu led by Kyōsō Shigetoshi, Ōtake Risuke's younger son. The honbu dojo is located at Iizasa Yasusada's home near the Katori shrine and is used by branches in good standing on special occasions. The Kyōsō Shibu regularly trains out of the Shisui Town Community Plaza and the Matsuyamashita Koen Sports Gymnasium in Inzai City.{{Cite web|url=http://katori-shinto-ryu.org/en/dojo/|title = Dojo – Tenshinsho-den Katori Shinto-ryu Kyoso Shibu}} Several branches have existed alongside or split from the mainline with varying ties to the Iizasa family, such as Noda, Yoseikan, Shinbukan, Sugino, Hatakeyama, Sugawara. ==Ranking and appointed positions== The different branches recognize different levels of Ranks and appointments. The traditional Ranks are a variant of the ''[[:en:menkyo|menkyo]]'' system.{{Cite web|url=http://judoinfo.com/Ranks/|title = Ranking Systems in Modern Japanese Martial Arts: Modern vs. Classical | Judo Info}} ===Ōtake=== ====Ranks==== * Mokuroku (目録, "catalog") * Menkyo (免許, "license, certificate") * Gokui Kaiden (極意皆伝, "deepest transmission") ====Appointments==== * Shidōsha (指導者, "mentor, coach"; given as license to teach outside of Shinbukan dojo) * Shihan (師範, 'instructor'; head-teacher) ===Sugino Dojo=== The Sugino line uses the modern [[Dan (Rank)|dan]] system, out of respect for Yoshio Sugino's (10th dan) judo teacher Kanō Jigorō, who was a pioneer of the dan-i Ranking system.{{Cite web|url=http://www.katsujinken.nl/2012/04/02/het-gradensysteem/|title = About the Katori Shinto ryu grading system | Katsujinken}} Sugino dojo members also receive densho/makimono. ===Hatakeyama-ha=== The Hatakeyama line (which has no current headmaster) uses the modern [[Dan (Rank)|dan]] system alongside the traditional ''menkyo'' system, issuing Ranks in both with shōden/chūden/okuden gradations to create equivalent Ranks from the ''menkyo'' system. ====Ranks==== *1st dan – kirikami shōden *2nd dan – kirikami chūden *3rd dan – mokuroku shōden *4th dan – mokuroku chūden *5th dan – menkyo mokuroku *6th dan – menkyo chūden *7th dan – menkyo okuden *8th dan – menkyo kaiden ===Sugawara-ha=== Sugawara Sogo Budo issues ''mokuroku'' and ''menkyo'' certifications. The highest level is menkyo kaiden. English-speaking members of Sugawara Budo refer to the Rank of menkyo as a "''kyōshi'' license" or ''menkyo kyōshi''. ===Noda-ha=== Noda Shinzan (1848–1917) began training in Katori Shintō-ryū in 1853 under Yamada Naomune, a retainer of the Date clan. Many Date retainers had trained in Katori Shintō-ryū and developed their own distinctive style. After Naomune's death in 1912, Shinzan enrolled his grandson, Seizan, under Yamaguchi Kumajiro, the most senior mainline shihan at the time. Following the deaths of Yamaguchi and his grandfather, Noda Seizan continued to train under Motomiya Toranosuke. After Motomiya's passing, Noda studied with Hayashi Yazaemon who began reforming and standardizing the mainline curriculum. Finding his style incompatible with Hayashi's, Noda Seizan quietly withdrew to private teaching, referring to his branch as Noda-ha Katori Shintō-ryū. Noda-ha preserves some kata that have been lost in the mainline. ===Ichigidō=== Shiigi Munenori began training in Katori Shintō-ryū under his father, who had trained with Motomiya Toranosuke, before formally training at Ōtake Risuke's dojo for over thirty years. He incorporates Katori Shintō-ryū as part of the curriculum of his Ichigidō organization and maintains a positive relationship with the Iizasa family. He writes "Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū" as "天真正伝香取神刀流" using '刀' (sword) in place of '道' (way). ===Yoseikan Budo=== Mochizuki Minoru, a judoka from the Kodokan who trained alongside Sugino Yoshio, has incorporated Katori Shintō-ryū into the curriculum of Yoseikan Budo. His son, Mochizuki Hiro, now heads Yoseikan. ==Notable practitioners== *[[:en:Yamazaki Susumu|Yamazaki Susumu]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=notes}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Further reading== * Amdur, Ellis (2002). ''Old School: Essays on Japanese Martial Traditions'', Edgework, p. 21–45 * Draeger, Donn F. ''The Martial Arts and Ways of Japan'' series, 3 volumes. * [[:en:Karl Friday|Friday, Karl F.]] (1997). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 1'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-378-8}} (Original Japanese title for all three volumes in this series is ''Mukei Bunkazai Katori Shinto-ryu'') * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). ''The Deity and the Sword – Katori Shinto-ryu Vol. 2'', Japan, Japan Publications Trading Co. {{ISBN|0-87040-405-9}} * Ōtake, Risuke (1977). Nippon Budokan Foundation. {{ISBN|9784583109848}} ([http://iss.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I027049263-00 description page]) * Ratti, Oscar & Westbrook, Adele (1973). ''Secrets of the Samurai, A Survey of the Martial Arts of Feudal Japan'', Charles E. Tuttle Co. {{ISBN|0-8048-0917-8}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (1997). ''Koryu Bujutsu, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 1, {{ISBN|1-890536-04-0}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (1999). ''Sword & Spirit, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 2, p. 67–69. {{ISBN|1-890536-05-9}} * Skoss, Diane (editor) (2002). ''Keiko Shokon, Classical Warrior Traditions of Japan'', Koryu Books, vol 3, {{ISBN|1-890536-06-7}} * Sugino, Yoshio & Ito, Kikue (1977). ''Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu Budo Kyohan'' (A Textbook of Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu Martial Training; originally published in 1941). * [[:en:Gordon Warner|Warner, Gordon]] & Draeger, Donn F. (1982). ''Japanese Swordsmanship: Technique And Practice'', {{ISBN|0-8348-0236-8}} ==External links== {{Portal|Japan|Martial Arts}} {{Commons Category}} *[https://shinbukan-katorishintoryu.org/ Shinbukan Dojo Official Website] *[http://katorishintoryu-uk.com/ Katori Shinto-ryu United Kingdom] *[http://katorishintoryu-usa.org/ Katori Shinto-ryu United States] *[http://katori-shinto-ryu.org/ Katori Shinto-ryu Kyōsō Shibu Official Website] *[http://katori-shintoryu.jp/en/main/ Katori Shinto-ryu Soke Official Website] {{navbox koryu}} {{Authority control}} ‎]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese martial arts]] }} [[en:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ast:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]] [[ca:Katori Shinto-ryu]] {{Commons category|Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū}} [[cs:Tenšin šóden katori]] [[da:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto-ryu]] [[de:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[es:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[fi:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]] [[fr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[he:טנשין שודן קאטורי שינטו ריו]] [[hu:Tensin sóden katori sintó-rjú]] [[id:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[it:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[ja:天真正伝香取神道流]] [[nl:Tenshin Shoden Katori Shinto Ryu]] [[no:Tenshinshoden Katori Shinto Ryu]] [[pl:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[pt:Tenshin shōden Katori shintō-ryū]] [[ru:Тэнсин Сёдэн Катори Синто-рю]] [[sv:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[tr:Tenshin Shōden Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[uk:Тенсин Седен Каторі Синто-рю]] [[vi:Katori Shintō-ryū]] [[zh:天真正傳香取神道流]] {{wikidata link|Q112025}}
